audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for journey to Jerusalem, daughters prophesying, Agabus binding sign, James/elders, purification, temple arrest, Roman rescue, Hebrew address setup.

verses.restored: When we had torn ourselves away from them and put out to sea, running straight we came to Cos, and the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara.
Finding a ship crossing to Phoenicia, we went aboard and set sail.
Coming in sight of Cyprus and leaving it on the left, we sailed to Syria and came down to Tyre, for there the ship was unloading cargo.
Finding the disciples, we remained there seven days. Through the Spirit they were saying to Paul not to go up to Jerusalem.
When our days were completed, we departed and went on, all of them with wives and children accompanying us until outside the city. Kneeling on the shore, we prayed.
Having said farewell to one another, we went up into the ship, and they returned to their own.
Completing the voyage from Tyre, we arrived at Ptolemais, greeted the siblings, and remained one day with them.
On the next day, departing, we came to Caesarea and entered the house of Philip the good-news announcer, who was one of the seven, and remained with him.
This one had four unmarried daughters who prophesied.
As we remained many days, a certain prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.

Coming to us and taking Paul's belt, he bound his own feet and hands and said, Thus says the holy Spirit: The man whose belt this is, the Judeans in Jerusalem will bind in this way and hand over into the hands of nations.
When we heard these things, both we and those local were urging him not to go up to Jerusalem.
Then Paul answered, What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart? For I am ready not only to be bound but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.
Since he was not persuaded, we became quiet, saying, Let the will of the Lord happen.
After these days, preparing, we went up to Jerusalem.
Some of the disciples from Caesarea also came with us, bringing us to Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, with whom we would lodge.
When we came to Jerusalem, the siblings received us gladly.
On the next day Paul went in with us to James, and all the elders were present.
Greeting them, he described one by one the things the Divine had done among the nations through his service.
Hearing, they glorified the Divine and said to him, You see, sibling, how many myriads there are among the Judeans who have trusted, and all are zealous for the Law.
They have been informed about you that you teach all the Judeans among the nations apostasy from Moses, telling them not to circumcise their children or walk in the customs.
What then is to be done? They will certainly hear that you have come.
Therefore do this that we say to you: there are four men with us having a vow upon themselves.

Take these and purify yourself with them, and pay expenses for them, so that they may shave the head, and all will know there is nothing to the things they have been informed about you, but that you yourself also walk guarding the Law.
But concerning those from the nations who have trusted, we wrote, judging that they should guard themselves from idol-offering, blood, strangled things, and sexual immorality.
Then Paul took the men, and the next day, being purified with them, entered the temple, announcing the completion of the days of purification until the offering was offered for each one of them.
When the seven days were about to be completed, the Judeans from Asia, seeing him in the temple, stirred up all the crowd and laid hands on him,

crying out, Men of Israel, help. This is the human teaching everyone everywhere against the people, the Law, and this place. And besides, he also brought Greeks into the temple and has made this holy place common.
For they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian with him in the city, whom they supposed Paul had brought into the temple.
The whole city was moved, and there was a running together of the people. Taking hold of Paul, they were dragging him outside the temple, and immediately the doors were shut.
As they were seeking to kill him, a report went up to the commander of the cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion.
At once taking soldiers and centurions, he ran down upon them. Seeing the commander and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
Then the commander, coming near, seized him and commanded him to be bound with two chains, and inquired who he might be and what he had done.
Some in the crowd were shouting one thing, some another. Not being able to know the certainty because of the uproar, he commanded him to be brought into the barracks.
When he came upon the stairs, it happened that he was carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the crowd.
For the multitude of the people followed, crying out, Take him away.
As he was about to be brought into the barracks, Paul says to the commander, Is it permitted for me to say something to you? He said, Do you know Greek?
Then you are not the Egyptian who before these days stirred up and led out into the wilderness the four thousand men of the Assassins?
Paul said, I am a Judean man from Tarsus of Cilicia, a citizen of no insignificant city. I ask you, permit me to speak to the people.
When he permitted him, Paul, standing on the stairs, motioned with the hand to the people. When there was great silence, he addressed them in the Hebrew dialect, saying:
